explanation:
**Normal health reference values** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. They are guidelines only; your personal medical history always takes priority.

## How It's Calculated

Melatonin dosage for sleep varies by age and sleep issue.

This calculator applies the formula with your values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to unusual values.

## Normal Reference Values

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting heart rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| SpO2 | 95-100% |
| Fasting glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guideline only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a specialized professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What is melatonin and how does it work?
faq.a: Melatonin is a hormone that regulates your circadian rhythm (sleep-wake cycle). It's not a sedative.

faq.q: How much melatonin should I take?
faq.a: The typical range is 0.3-5 mg. More isn't better—as little as 0.5 mg can be as effective as 5 mg.
Take it 30-60 minutes before you want to sleep.
It's effective for jet lag, shift work sleep problems, and circadian rhythm sleep disorders.

faq.q: What are the side effects of melatonin?
faq.a: Common side effects include headache, daytime sleepiness, and vivid dreams.
Melatonin should only be used in children with a doctor's prescription.

faq.q: Can you become dependent on melatonin?
faq.a: No tolerance or dependence has been reported with melatonin use. However, long-term safety data is limited.

faq.q: When should I talk to my doctor about melatonin?
faq.a: Consult your doctor if you experience unusual symptoms, if melatonin isn't working, or if you have a family history of cardiovascular or metabolic disease.
